title: Another Age of Anxiety: Psychological Distress and the 'Asset Economy'
author: Davies

opening_move.description: Rates of anxiety disorder have risen sharply since 2008, especially among young people, yet sociology and critical theory have said surprisingly little about this — focusing on depression as the neoliberal pathology while neglecting anxiety as a distinct phenomenon with its own sociological logic.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Taking anxiety seriously as a distinct sociological phenomenon, rather than collapsing it into depression, reveals a different dimension of contemporary capitalism — specifically the 'asset economy' and its generation of a pathological orientation toward an open, speculative future.

rivals_engaged: critical theory treatment of depression (Fisher et al.)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper distinguishes anxiety from depression as a sociological object, argues that medicalization has obscured its existential dimensions, and proposes the 'asset economy' — a financialized form of neoliberalism organized around balance sheets and anticipation rather than production — as the key sociological context for contemporary anxiety.

epistemic_mode.quantitative_methods: Statistical data on rising rates of anxiety disorder diagnoses (WHO, UK/US epidemiological data, Goodwin et al., Slee et al.) used to motivate the sociological analysis.
data_sources: Goodwin et al. (2020), Slee et al. (2021) UK/US diagnostic rate data
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Genealogical analysis of psychiatric history (DSM, neo-Kraepelinian turn); conceptual analysis of 'asset economy' literature (Adkins, Konings, Christophers); Foucauldian reading of diagnostic governance.
epistemic_mode.notes: Statistical data opens and motivates the paper but does not constitute the core argument. The argument is sociological-theoretical: the asset economy generates a pathological temporality of anticipation that accounts for the anxiety epidemic. Evidence role is illustrative/supportive.

normative_stake.domain: social theory of mental health, critique of financialized capitalism

confidence_notes: The paper occupies an interesting epistemic position: it opens with quantitative epidemiological data (anxiety rates) but the core argument is theoretical-sociological. The data is illustrative/supportive. The key move is proposing the asset economy — a specific form of financialized neoliberalism — as the sociological context for anxiety, which is a normative-diagnostic argument about what kind of capitalism produces what kind of pathological subjectivity.
